does: This paper addresses machine unlearning in federated learning by proposing FedCCCU, a fairness-aware federated unlearning method that explicitly handles two core problems: unfair performance degradation when approximate unlearning techniques affect uninvolved clients' retained data, and unrealistic evaluation under synthetic data assumptions. The authors conduct comprehensive benchmarking of existing federated unlearning methods under realistic data heterogeneity conditions and fairness metrics, then introduce their approach which uses cross-client constraints to balance the competing objectives of removing target client data while preserving model utility for other participants.
connects: This work builds on the intersection of machine unlearning and federated learning literatures. It extends prior federated unlearning research that primarily focused on efficiency through gradient ascent or distillation-based approximate methods, but largely ignored fairness implications. The paper responds to broader critiques of federated learning evaluation that rely on oversimplified IID and non-IID synthetic benchmarks rather than realistic data heterogeneity. It connects to fairness-aware federated learning research that addresses client-level performance disparities, and to the growing machine unlearning literature emphasizing practical, privacy-preserving deletion beyond simple retraining approaches.
enables: This work opens pathways toward practical federated unlearning systems that respect both deletion rights and fairness constraints in real-world deployments. By establishing realistic evaluation benchmarks with genuine data heterogeneity, it enables future research to build more reliable unlearning methods. The fairness-aware framework could extend to other federated learning problems where local data retention conflicts with global objectives. The paper positions federated unlearning as critical infrastructure for privacy-regulated ML systems and enables organizations to implement GDPR-like deletion rights without imposing disparate computational or performance costs across client populations.
